I'm super excited for this presentation because I feel like it is something that is talked about a lot in the Gym Legacy group and it is very relevant to what I experience on a daily basis and so I felt like I really hope this shed light on a topic that I feel a lot of people don't understand, which is what happens when you scale your business. And the reason that I think a lot of you don't understand that what you're going through is normal is because it hasn't been presented to you as normal and you haven't done it before. And so I would like to put some of your worries at ease and for you to have a better understanding of the normal things that happen in any business when you're growing it. And so what you must understand first is that in order to scale your business, then the old business must die. The old way of doing things is going down the drain, you're doing completely new things. And when you change everything in the business, you have to understand that before you are granted the success that you're so excited for, you are going to encounter failure. It's going to feel like crap. There are going to be things that go wrong. And so I want to share with you the normal pitfalls that you're going to encounter that are going to feel like failure. But I would like to call these growing pains because they are truly the normal things that happen in any business as it scales. One, the CEO holding on to tightly. Two, having the wrong people. Three, focus on marketing and sales, not retention. And then four is not tracking metrics. And so I want to break each of one of these down for you guys. And please know, these are not all of the growing pains that you will encounter. These are just the ones that I see as the most common in my business, in businesses I study, in businesses of friends, in your businesses. So the CEO holding on to tightly. What happens is you start hiring people, but you don't actually delegate. You delegate tasks, but not decision making power. And so what that does is you actually end up more stressed than you were before. So you start hiring these people, and then you get there one day, and you walk in, and you're like, why do I even employ these people? Like, I'm more stressed than I was when it was just me. It's harder than it was when it was just me. That's probably because you're not actually delegating any authority. You're not actually delegating any decision making. And so everyone just does all these things and comes to you to approve it, make a decision, make a decision, make a decision, and you're distracted all of the time. I want you to know that's not abnormal, but it's also not how you should be doing things. It's something you identify that you're doing so that you can fix it. And so that's what happens is you start hiring and you feel more pressure, not less. And what I want you to understand is that you should actually feel relieved when you hire people onto your team. You hire for relief. You do not hire to become more stressed. You invest in the beginning and make sure that they have context and can make decisions and everything else that we talk about. But ultimately, you should feel better once someone joins your team. And so you have to trace this back to if you are not delegating enough, if you are only delegating tasks and checklists rather than authority and decision making power. And so what you are ultimately doing is bottlenecking the growth of your company out of fear. You are fearful that someone can't make the right decision. You are fearful that if they don't go through you, then they're going to choose something wrong. And this comes down to a number of things. But if you have the right person, which you have to ask yourself, like, is this the right person to be making these decisions? Right? Then there's no reason that you shouldn't be delegating it to them, right? And so then it comes down again, like, are you the wrong leader or is this the wrong team? That's a question you have to ask yourself. And what I also would like you to understand is that all CEOs are scared to let go. You need to let go anyways. And you will only get fucked one out of five times. And so I tell you this, and I know that some of you will let go and let someone make decisions and let things stop micromanaging things so tightly. And some of you will get fucked from it. And I'm OK with that because it's only probably one out of five times that it's going to happen. Happens to me all the time, OK? There's one out of five times. I know that if I delegate something or I give this away, somebody's going to mess it up. That's just the cost of doing business. And the entire point of hiring people is to relieve you. So it makes no sense to put the team on your back and carry them. Remember, you do not want to be the genius with a thousand helpers. There's not who you want to be. And this is one of the most obvious and probably the most common things that I see when people are scaling in businesses, like they just can't delegate authority or decision making power. And the people who stand on the stage and have a seven figure sword are the ones who do, OK? And again, why are you paying people to make you more stressed? You are literally hiring people and then not letting them do their jobs. It doesn't make any sense, right? So stop freezing out of fear and let go. Two, you have the wrong people. OK, I want you to think about it like this. When you build a machine to do one thing, say you want that machine to climb up the stairs, right? I think I'm like engineering. You cannot then ask the machine to do a new thing without new parts or repairs. So if I built a machine, I just needed to climb up the stairs and then I start to want to do something else. I'm like, I want that machine to be able to spin on ice. Well, then I look at the machine and I'm like, hmm, in order to spin on ice, I don't know if these parts are going to be able to do that. I think I need some new parts. I also think I need to switch them out, right? OK, you cannot expect that your entire team will be the team that scales your business with you. That is not your fault. That is not their fault. That is the reality of what happens when you grow up business. And it's something that you must accept. You cannot hold on to the thought that everyone will be with you forever. That's not reality, right? It's especially relevant to small businesses, right? Because when you decide to scale up your business, you may upset some of the team you currently have. And you can do your best. You can do all of the culture meetings one on one. You can present it the right way. And some people just won't care. Some people will just have it stuck in their mind. They're going to say, all we care about now is money. When is enough enough? It's not like it used to be. We got too big, too fast. OK, those are people who are like, that is that is normal. You're going to hear that. OK. And so I just want you to know that that is to be expected. And if it has not happened yet, it will happen if you are grown. And so you do your best to transparently manage and explain all the changes. But at the end of the day, you often need to get some new teammates to get some next level. This is something you should be transparent with your team about. We had our team meet up just two months ago and something I told everyone in the room was that. According to statistics, 90% of you won't be here in four years. And I told them that because I would like to be an anomaly and I would like for them to be there in four years. But the reality is that when you change expectations so often and so much to accommodate the marketplace, there are people who do not understand. Right. And so you can hope and you can do everything in your power. But there is much of the time where you need to accept that you need to get a new teammate, get a new team to get to the next level. And especially if you have not installed still the strong culture around change. So that is why it is so important that with your core values or core tenants, whatever you want to call them, that you put in something there around embracing change. And it's not something that you just put on the wall and say, we embrace change, we embrace change, but like you embrace change. You're the first one to embrace change and to look at it as growth and as a healthy business rather than as something to scare or shy away. Right. And so you cannot be afraid to let people go. You cannot be afraid to hire from the outside or to start fresh. That's obviously a worst case scenario, but I have seen it where someone's culture is so terrible in one direction that they basically hired people for a culture that is inhibiting their growth. And so they can't change the people. So they have to change the team. Right. And then thinking of I just want you to remember, like, if you are letting someone go because there is no way they're going to make it in the new business, it is not just for the sake of your business, but for the sake of them. Because if you know that they are no longer somebody that will move this business forward, each day that you keep them on your team, they could have been having a new job establishing their career there. Right. And so they have no path forward in your business. It's really selfish to keep them on your team just because you're afraid to let them go. So short term, it might be painful, might feel like ripping off a bandaid, but long term, it's going to avoid the lingering pain where you're just sitting there every day, day and day out, like this person is not on board. This person doesn't know how to do this. This person just can't accommodate the growth. Like, man, he was so good and we had only 100 members now with 250. You just really can't keep up. If you're thinking of those things all the time, you need to make a change. Okay. And the thing is a lot of entrepreneurs get used to those thoughts. You need to stop and stop tolerating mediocrity because if you tolerate mediocrity, you will have a mediocre business. Third, focusing on marketing, not retention. This is a mistake a lot of first time business owners make. And it's honestly just their lack of attention to retention instead of marketing. It also has to do with their having the inability to focus on multiple priorities. Some people are like, well, what's your number one priority? And it's like, they're all number one. And that's the reality of as your business gets bigger, they're all number one priorities, which is why we preach. You need to have a team because each person needs a different priority, right? It's easier for people to have that as a focus, but you cannot lose one or the other. And why is this so detrimental? Aside from the fact that it's obviously ridiculous because it costs so much less to retain a customer than it does to acquire a new one. I want to show you from my point of view, from an internal perspective, what it does. It will ruin your culture. It will discourage your team and ultimately kill your reputation. And so I say that because if you speak to anyone who works at any company where the churn is high, you just hear everything. The CEOs don't give a shit about the customer. CEOs don't know what's going on. They have no idea what they're doing. I don't understand what's going on. They basically lose faith in the leader because they feel that you don't have the client's best interest in mind. Right? And if you don't have the client's best interest in mind, how do you think your team views the clients? If you don't care, you have to care more than anyone. OK? And you have to care about your team and about your clients. And so it's like you're putting your team in a bad spot when you're not focusing on retention. Because it's your responsibility to direct their attention. And so you need to direct it towards their retention. Otherwise they'll feel like, I don't know why he's not paying attention to this. Does he not see the hole in the bucket? Like blah, blah. And they get really discouraged because like they're doing all this work to get people into a gym, to give them good service, blah, blah. Ultimately to lose the business. Right? And they had a part in building that. They have a part in building this community. And so it's honestly a culture killer to the max. OK? And then lastly, like it obviously is going to kill your reputation because if people are leaving your gym consistently, like there's a reason they're leaving and they're not saying good things. OK? So you always want to make sure like these are not quantifiable, but they are like the intangible. Like the underlying. I don't know if I want to say like ninjas of the dark, like something like they're going to be what kills your business, not the stats. Right? The you see the numbers and the numbers might be bad, but this is the stuff that's actually going to happen if you have too much attrition. OK? And so don't be the first time business owner that only focuses on sales, only services, the customers that are new. Like you have to be the person who pays attention like how will our lifetime customers take this as well as our new customers? Right? And as you're growing and scaling, you can't forget to take care of those people. Right? Like I always think to myself like, OK, how will this change affect? Oh, geez. That's what I say. And how will it affect newcomers? Right? Remember, your members are. Your cultural co-founders. So you need to over deliver to them, especially in times of change, especially if you're changing things at your gym, you know, host them in a private meeting, have a little members advisory board where they get to give their input every quarter on to like the gym and the changes that are being made. This is your chance to explain your vision to them, explain why you're making the change you are and to be transparent about it. OK? Over deliver. Lastly, not tracking metrics. OK? So as you're growing, your business is it's really easy to get distracted with people problems instead of number problems. So in the beginning, it's like cash flow, numbers, sales. And then once you kind of get those things like a hold of them, then it starts like, OK, now there are always people problems and you start forgetting about the numbers. Right? However, when you are doing this, this is when you must develop the habit of knowing and understanding your metrics. OK? This is literally the time when it is the most important because scaling will cost you money and it will cost you time for it gives you money and time. And therefore you need to be tracking all these business metrics so you can watch the trends so you know where things are going. And then you can look three months from now and say, OK, we made that change. And now I see the results of it. Things are going up three months later. Right? And so what you need to do as you scale is develop the habit of tracking on the metrics and then delegate responsibility of each metric to the appropriate teammate and educate them on that metric. Obviously, I've talked about this in the entire leadership portal, but I am saying it again because it's so dang important. You're not the only one that should be tracking all these metrics. Your team should be responsible for them. OK. And as you grow, you need people who are going to push you when a metric is off. You might have 20 numbers in front of you every day. You need someone to say, this one's been trending down or this one's been trending up and they need to be the person to point it out. Right. And so if you want to get everything out of a certain role from someone on your team, then everything must be accounted for, including hitting the metrics. Right. You haven't fully delegated the role of sales manager unless they are managing the numbers of sales. Right. Think about it. And not only that, but allowing your team to account for different metrics is appreciated by all of them because of the transparency. I can't tell you how many times new people come into our team and they're like, I'm so appreciative of the transparency here. You guys share all the financials, all the numbers, everything, whether it's good or bad. And that gives that's you as a business owner extending trust to your team. So if you have any hesitations about it, just know that regardless one, they don't actually like if you think the numbers are bad, your team is going to be like, yeah, those are probably not that good. But like end of the day, your team can get a job and you're the one stuck with the business. So like if you feel like your numbers are atrocious, like they don't feel that bad about it because like they can get a job and they can go do something else. And I just hope that that makes you feel better because it may feel like the end of the world to you, but that's because you own the business. And that's why you signed up for it. Right. So I would like to leave you with grow or die. Remember, it's not easy to scale a business. It is a test of persistence and you will feel like you're failing a lot of the time. And I just want you to know, like it's okay to feel that way. And it's normal. What's not normal is being happy every day. What's not normal is feeling like you're a success all the time. Like you might have a week where you feel super successful and then you might have three weeks where you feel like crap and then another week of success. And that's just kind of how business goes. And so accepting that and knowing it and being able to identify it gives you more power than rejecting it ordinarily.
